video_title: Supercycle = Gold & Silver to 7X. Jeff Currie with Steven Feldman on Open Position
transcript: You know, I I don't like to get into the numbers game, but you know, I I could see 10,000 gold and $300 silver. I don't think that's an unrealistic. By the way, and people that put out these numbers, oh my god, he's crazy. Every super cycle we've been through in the one in the 70s and one in the 2000s, the price go of these commodities go up by 7x. I want to talk about some of your personal activities for a second, but we we skidded past an asset class that's in the commodity space and I mentioned it before, gold and to a lesser extent silver which has a monetary and an industrial use. Um is is that in your purview? Do you have a point of view about the that as a lot of our again a lot in the audience a lot of folks in the audience have exposure are very interested um you know it it makes more headlines than lithium does and I'm just curious if you have a point of view. >> Oh I'm I'm in extremely bullish on on all of the precious and um the core of it really is the debasement meaning that we we we fiat currencies have only been around since 1971 and um so it's a short experiment in the history of mankind and you're going to go oh these central banks are not they're not going to go back to the gold standard but what's happening is the gold price goes up their reserves become all gold back so it's not like they they all a sudden wake up we're going to be back on the gold standard no the price of gold will take care of that and the banks that don't have the gold are scrambling to catch up to get the gold. And so that's why I'm a big believer that, you know, the upside there on gold. The upside on silver is really part of, you know, this the solar um solar panel story. And that's why China was, you know, buying them, buying as much silver as it possibly could to protect that industry, which it dominates. But hey, after after what's going on um with the straight of moves, the demand for solar is going to keep going up. So I, you know, I I mean, we're really still very restricted on supply. Um, you know, I I don't like to get into the numbers game, but you know, I I could see 10,000 gold and $300 silver. I don't think that's an unrealistic. By the way, and people had put out these numbers, OH MY GOD, he's crazy. Every super cycle we've been through in the one in the 70s and one in the 2000s, the price go of these commodities go up by 7x on average, >> right? You know, I I by the way, I I am people know I have uh Wealthon is actually a sister business to a physical precious metals company. We should talk about Abacks. I'd love to be your partner in that. We talk about a lot of our uh audience talks about the cyclicality even of the metal and like like like and I come back and I say, well, it's a monetary asset. There's almost $40 trillion of debt and interest rates are high and I'm not even sure, you know, how the pendulum swings the other way. There's there's no some problems have no solution other than the basement. And so we in this channel over and over and over we say what we're trying we're not trying to debunk narratives. We're trying to show the math the and the math doesn't show up immediately. But I can't even construct how $40 trillion of debt and a permanently long higher for longer interest rate regime can possibly ever work. the math you can't no one has been able I've asked I've asked the listeners I said just show me the math about where taxes have to go or where inflation has to go or where spending has to go where 40 doesn't become 50 doesn't become 60 which feeds into your case of why gold isn't going to be 4,000 forever there's there is no math case and I I keep saying well if you want to tell me tell me the math case where it goes down to 50% of GDP and the case for gold gets destroyed it It doesn't it doesn't exist. And we as a channel and also as a company, we're not gloom and doomers. We don't think the world ends. But we also think that and that that the that the dollar can lose its value and will continue to lose its value because that's the way you get out of the debt problem until there's a crisis. That's not going to change. And when the crisis comes, that's going to be great for gold certainly in in the in the in the in short to medium to even very very long run because it'll its brand will elevate. Um, so thank you for that. I appreciate it. I know the audience does too. I won't hold you to the numbers. It's not a when question. 10,000 10,000 will happen. It's just I don't know what. I wish I knew. I just I wish I knew. C can we go back to uh again a theme we talk a lot about because it not only has uh an investing piece but it also has it could be it could be the doom piece it could be the doom for society piece which is AI and you know I think a lot of investors are focusing on the investment and people the S&P is now an AI S&P it's a market weighted and the hyperscalers have driven the market um so there's a lot more at stake than just hey we're going to all be able to be more productive that's the tool then there's the investment element of it which feels boom and bust I'd like you to comment on it and then there's the societal piece of it which is you know I I've been writing this in LinkedIn I've been writing it for wealthy on you know uh AI takes your brain robots take your back if the AI thing busts it's going to take your wealth and nobody voted for this you know and it's going to create all sorts of societal friction and pain and yet the lords of AI will get richer and richer and if the market crashes they'll just buy everything cheaply. Um that could be a little cynical. I I I understand it and I'm and by the way I'm I'm not necessarily cynical. I'm just setting forth a case which frankly I happen to believe but okay. What's your holistic view of AI from productivity tool to societal threat to investment opportunities and and and risks? >> I I I think there's one part of this that is missing from all the discussions is they take the standard tech model which is infinitely scalable at zero marginal cost. meaning that you know Microsoft built some software and that was your fixed cost doesn't you don't your your marginal cost to replicate it is super cheap and then you just roll it out to the rest of the world and you don't get any type of um you it you know prices go down the world adopts to it what they're missing in this AI is no longer that infinitely scalable at zero marginal cost because you're going to consume raw material materials and if you consume the raw materials your supply curve slopes up like anything else in the world and it runs into walls all the time. In fact, what you hear from, you know, different users of of AI is we look at the price of AI compute, it's hitting that it's hitting the that physical floor where the commodity price can drive it back up. So, you know, I like to point this out, you know, about the you know, the humans versus the AI. All the commodities that drive the AI compute are not renewable. The um the human being is renewable. So it should end up being cheaper because all we're doing is we're going to try to replace humans with commodities. Um and so yeah, I don't think that, you know, that that's going to end up being, you know, unless you're out in space. And I I had this argument over the weekend, the space, you know, if you're out there and you're using, you know, solar um power, the problem is like the cooling of these things out there, you know, um in fact, the they go, "Oh, well, SpaceX going to launch one." Yeah, it's four times the cost of one on terrestrial world, and we probably already overbuilt the terrestrial world. Um but by the way, in commodities, we went through all this ourselves with, you know, mining in space. So, um, you know, we I've heard this before myself, but I think the I think the part that's missing here is it's going to be a lot more expensive than what people in the tech world have experienced before because they're now cyclical commodities because AI compute, it's a cyclical commodity. And you know the point I say too is you look at the price of AI compute um it's actually going down now which is an you know indication you may have overbuilt already uh but it's not going high so how are you going to justify going to space and spending four times the cost of the ones down here on the in terrestrial world uh at the point when the AI compute price itself is going down there's so I you know am I worried about it taking over the and all these other issues. No, it's going to be a tool like a calculator that we use. Um, and we'll figure out how to use it really efficient and it's going to, you know, open up, you know, a world of new opportunities, but it's not going to, you know, you it can replace jobs that are repetitive and that don't need verification. When it needs verification, um, you got to have a human there. But like the point I like to say is you look at radiology, it should be the one industry that would have gotten killed by AI because they they they could do it in in radiology going back for I think 30 years. And yeah, we hire more radiologists today than at any other point in time because you need to verify it. >> You know, I I I I'll belabor the point. Um, you know, I look at all these technologies and let's go back to the tractor which moved uh farmers to cities and they got city jobs and then you move forward to something in our generation. You know, you had e-commerce, the internet and e-commerce and that took bricks and mortar stores and moved them online but people could still work on online retailers. I'm a bit less sanguin about something that takes your brain and because it's not putting it anywhere. It's not saying, "Listen, I'm going to take your brain. Your body will follow. We're going to put it in a certain indifferent place." I could see the winners of AI hiring more people. Uh because they will have some productivity advantage, they will have capital advantage, and they will be able to do more. But I'm not sure where it's moving people. I I know this is a terrible example, but I read an article that militaries no longer need sharpshooters. So, the idea that a a drone does what a sharpshooter used to do. The sharpshooter is now gone. That job is now gone. It is a gone job. It's it's not the world's greatest example. I mean, but you know, right now lawyers are at risk and you have a lot of white collar people who live in their, you know, their their jobs is in their brain and it's going to you could say, well, okay, that'll elevate them to do more value added work because all their associates will be AI and yes, you can't go to a court without verifying it. And that may be great for the biggest law firms who can hire more lawyers and have better pyramids and their whole pyramids are AI agents, but what happens to those middle market law firms, the ones that were doing the middle market work, where do those guys all go? And so I am I listen I I'm not necessarily saying I'm right but I am saying that there is a debate that is not being had that needs to be had because I think in large part government has been a little bit co-opted by the same people that need to be regulated. They're the people donating, getting it used to be the crypto people. Now it's going to be the AI people who are supporting the candidates, who are becoming the governors, who are becoming the Congress people, even the presidents who are not having this conversation. Now, I also think it has an investing uh and you you brought this up. You bought Microsoft because it had an absurd return on investment capital because their marginal cost and their margins were enormous. And now they're a railroad company. You know, they're basically building the same thing, the railroad company. And when will they be trading at the railroad multiple? I mean, they're still great companies. You know, those railroads are great monopolies. Again, my infrastructure experience says those are great businesses to run and you want to own them for the long run, but they shouldn't be trading at 50 pees. >> Yeah, exactly. and and there has to be some relation to book value. And uh anyway, so listen, you're now an oil man, right? You're in the oil business. >> Uh tell us a little bit about that. I think there's a lot of people with the fantasy, me included. At least I did it. I left Wall Street. >> I didn't I didn't go uh I didn't go to another asset manager. I made the fateful decision to become an owner. And then I uh no one told me that I would never sleep again. Uh being an entrepreneur is a little bit like being a parent. Everyone tells you it's great and then you do it and it's pretty good, but it's got its ups and downs and then you're done and you may not want to you may not want to do it again. You had the experience. Same thing with entrepreneurship. So tell me about uh 1947 oil and gas. >> Well, it's a it's a shallow water Louisiana light suite. um production. So, it's in the Gulf of Mexico in the shallow water. It's a The way I described it when we found the asset is oil is the cheapest thing on your screen and within the oil, this is the cheapest thing out there. You know, stuff's trading like one and a half times earnings. Um and there's some structural issues um why everything in the water is is trading, you know, at very very low levels. But the and there's another part of this that's happens is because the stuff in the water it has to be abandonment cost of the you know the rigs out there and stuff like that and way the the accounting is it forces you to write that off up front while you discount the the the value in the future. So, it's just kind of a a artificial um derating of this space. And there were some bad operators who did some bad things in the Gulf, too, that gave it a bad name. But, nonetheless, these things are, you know, at, you know, where are we? $70 oil. These things are very, very cash flow accreative. And I think the the, you know, the way actually I asked Claude the other day, I go, who's who's richer? um Elon Musk or JD Rockefeller. His answer was, well, as a share of US GDP, they're about the same. However, Rockefeller was a different kind of rich, meaning he had cash. And you see all these guys that are in the in um you know, in the tech business, they're all making their way to the Middle East to go ask for cash. And part of that has to do with the fact that um commodities and oil and all these industries generate cash. They pay dividends. Like you just saying, they pay dividends. And this is why I don't understand is you're buying these these hyperscalers that pay, you know, they have 0% free cash flow versus these these oil majors at 15.5. Um, you know, so the the the point here is, you know, you look at these assets, they're in the tail, and so they don't have much of a decline rate, and ultimately they end up paying out a good dividend. And I think that, you know, that that was my initial traction to to the space. Um but I think the the the other point too is we can roll up and find a lot more deals like this because um you know there's been an abandonment of that part of the US production. Part of it is that they're too small um to be of interest to um like private equity and they'd already been discarded from the different majors. So the only solution here is you got to roll them up into bigger players that you know could be of interest to other part parts of the world. So I'm super excited about it. It's very interesting. I want to tie this back to the comment of of you know what our audience is looking to do. There is uh cash flow is a very underrated uh undertalked about uh metric for investing. And by the way, even for my exposure to uh to the conventional stock market, which of course I am, uh one of my metrics, I'm not an S&P 500 investor, but in my investing, and again, everyone's heard the audience has heard me say this a million times, I'm looking for different metrics, return on investing capital, free cash flow, even if they're not sharing it with me, but the best investments are free cash flow shared with me. and hence why I'm in the pipeline investing business. But if you can get into a private business that it's so interesting as a private business owner that are that it's cash flowing, you start to care a little bit less that it's always growing by 7%. You just care about the absolute amount and if the check comes, it's like a big victory. God bless. You know, that's what you pay your kids' tuitions with. That's what you pay your mortgage with. That's what you pay your insurance with. that's how you pay your car bills. And you know that simple fact is almost universally misunderstood. The second thing is this notion of hard assets. Um you talked about Rockefeller. Interestingly the first Forbes 400 was uh the guy who was number one it was in the 80s or 80ish something. His was Daniel Lewig. He was worth $4 billion in 1980 something and he owned uh shipping containers and ports and other infrastructure around global shipping. And he was worth $4 billion and the book value of that was $4 billion. And it created according to legend or lore he was getting $400ish million dollar a year of cash. And that's a lot of money even if at that t tax rate in the in the pre- Reagan tax rate. So uh you know I'm not saying that people shouldn't shoot for growth and the other way of making a lot of money as an American investor is to be long innovation uh but not to overpay for it but not to have something that is uh in that ven diagram that gives you exposure to real assets that gives you exposure to commodities which are in scarce supply and gives you a cash flow is something that people should think long and hard about of having more of not less. By the way, we're IPOing our company a 1947 um here on the London name. So it, you know, it'll be a publicly traded entity in um >> Okay. >> Yeah. sometime in the month of July. >> Does it have an indicative dividend yield? >> It does actually. I I don't know it off the top of my head, but I do know that I know that the the title of the piece that the research guys that put it out, they tied it titled it um shallow water, deep value. So, I like that title. >> Well, well, if you if you send us the research report, we can we can abstract it and send it to the audience if they're interested and they can take a look. So listen, I told you uh before we came on that there are a couple of personal questions that we like to ask um you know punditry is an interesting thing uh because we're sort of talking about markets but we're not so much talking about ourselves and you're an important guy and you've been in kneedeep in very important markets in very important places Goldman Sachs Carlile uh and you know you you're you're watched listened and respected by a lot of people. So, let me ask you, uh, what's worrying you? What's keeping you up at night? >> Some type of technological innovation that changes the need for an ability to produce these hard assets so that these cycles stop. And um the pricing the the pricing framework which these things um um they they depend on one thing in commodities. You can always forecast supply and technological innovation. And the minute we can't forecast that, that starts to become in you know some difficult because the the technological innovation is you know very um it's got a it very linear in most of these commodities growth to it. So I I would say that that's the one that um that keeps me up at night cuz I can tell you demand collapsing or something like that won't derail these stories. um you know it has to it really has to come from from the supply side. If demand collapsed you just push it out a couple years like it remember 09 you know 0809 happened uh we lost 24 months but >> yeah and the and the pandemic. >> Yeah. Yeah. >> So is is is there something on your radar? Is it nuclear fusion? Is it you know getting something into the atmosphere that you know collects solar in a different way? Is there something that you see now that >> the fusion the fusion I I I assume we're going to get there. Um but that's part of the the electric story. By the way, ultimately the the story on on oil and this and I think there's one point that I think people don't understand about China and you know the renewables, nuclear power and batteries. That's like the railroads in the United States in, you know, the 1870s. Once they connected the Pacific with the Atlantic with the railroads, uh, it was game over for the rest of the world. The first person who can build out their entire power grid with zero marginal cost, meaning just renewables, batteries, and nuclear power wins. And the Americans and Europeans are so sleep at the wheel. They're arguing about this stuff like it has something to do with the environment. No, it has nothing to do with the environment. It has everything to do with getting to a grid that has zero marginal cost. And I hear all these politicians in the US going, "Oh, but this stuff, these renewables cost seven times. Who cares?" Because once you have it and you got the batteries attached to it, um, you're good to go. And China is, you know, and so if you throw fusion into the mix on this thing, boom, somebody's going to get there just almost overnight. And I say China is >> is somebody in the United States going to get there? Is this what Elon Musk is doing through SpaceX and Tesla or is it all so desperate and everyone's trying to get their small isolated piece and it, you know, maybe it connects, maybe it doesn't connect. >> Yeah. Yeah. I mean, we're talking about power grid on the ground to go out and be able to, you know, power everything because it's your manufacturing cost basis of everything just goes to zero. And then thing that came out of the Ukraine in Iran was oil's dead regardless of whatever happens in the long run is the one thing that came out of this is war machine 1.0 meaning artificial muscle tanks and um you know all the oil driven stuff um that stuff can't hold a candle to the AI drone um war machine war kit 2.0 O and >> the the thing that's very clear is those big refuelers, those planes and those big um ships are vulnerable to the drones. You need batteries. So little batteries. See like that. You know, the Germans overbuilt all that renewable capacity. They just lack the battery technology. Now that military is 100% focusing on building out battery technology, we'll get there so quick. I was at this climate week thing and everybody's oh the climate the environmental oh these defense guys are getting all of our money. I said who created all of those environmental technologies, nuclear power, solar, wind, batteries, all of that comes out of defense. So now defense is gunning to build battery packs like that big that you know are not vulnerable. Um we're going to go in lightning speed to get these batteries up to speed. If you do that everything is going to be electrified. Um, so I I I would put nuclear fusion into that mix. >> All right, la last question and we'll let you get back to your business day. Well, you're in London, so it's a little later. Um, so you've had a long career. Uh, I'm sure you've been quite successful, although I'm not going to look at your brokerage statement. And so today, as a guy with some wealth and some f freedom and flexibility, what what are you solving for personally? What are you trying to make happen that would make you content and happy? >> Oh, that that's a really a good question. Um, you know, basically, um, spending time and being connected with everybody else around me. And, um, you know, that's the one thing that, you know, we were both at Goldman. We we learned how to become disconnected from the world. Um, being more connected is what what I'm solving to be. >> That's good to hear. you know, I I would I if I've listened to everybody's answer, I've refined my own. Uh and I think it's something that everybody should do, but it changes over a long life, which is to be structurally aligned with your values. And um I think when you get to our age, you have that luxury. and uh you know the time you see less time on the clock maybe you have some money in your pocket and all of a sudden you don't want to be with some people or in some situations uh and you don't want to have that uncomfortable feeling that you had when we were young at Goldman and trying to achieve for different things like your kids 529 and not getting fired. Uh now it's just it's just flow. If somebody decided that they wanted to fire me, I would just find something else to do that fit my my my uh my rubric. Anyway, Jeff, this has been uh terrific. We don't have enough folks who have your depth of knowledge. I would encourage you if you can, if there's anything you think the audience would be interested, if you're still writing research and it's not proprietary or behind a payw wall, we would love to send it. So, um again, thank you. And if there's ever uh that technology that changes and keeps you up at night, we'll have you definitely back on the show, but hopefully before that. >> Great. Thank you for having me. It was a pleasure. >> Thank you. Jeff Curry, 27 years at Goldman, the Halo Framework, the commodity super cycle, and now a producing oil company in the Gulf of America. Jeff, this was exactly the conversation I was hoping for. Thank you for being on Open Position.
